body.title: Consequence check: difficulty tier routing (retroactive — D-target-difficulty-tiers)
body.result: Observed all 3 effects. Effect 1 (default difficulty=1 makes legacy/test tickets Igor-eligible): OCCURRED — T-test-ticket proves it, pe_chain fails on missing description as secondary guard. Effect 2 (adopt_next_ticket): CLEAR — T-adopt-next-uses-cmd-next shipped. Effect 3 (higher-difficulty unclaimed): STRUCTURAL — no automated higher-tier consumer, CC fills manually, no backlog alarm.
body.description:
**What changed:** D-target-difficulty-tiers-2026-05-19 added target_difficulty field + --max-difficulty filtering + IGOR_MAX_DIFFICULTY=1 daemon config so Igor only claims Apprentice-tier tickets.
**Predicted unintended effects:** (1) Tickets without target_difficulty default to 1 — test fixtures and legacy tickets become Igor-eligible, defeating the filter. (2) adopt_next_ticket was not updated to use cmd_next, so Igor still picks up any sprint ticket regardless of difficulty filter. (3) Higher-difficulty tickets sit unclaimed indefinitely if no higher-tier consumer is wired up.
**Signals to watch:** Channel: no more `[pe_chain] ✗ T-test-ticket` or similar bare-ticket failures after the chain ships. `cc_queue.py list` shows igor-worker tickets are all difficulty=1. Tickets with difficulty>1 have a non-igor consumer picking them up or remain in sprint with expected pending state.
**Gate condition:** Check by 2026-06-03 or when T-igor-difficulty-cap closes.

**Affected files:** None — observation and verification only
**Design rules:** none apply
**Scope boundary:** Observe predicted effects; annotate outcome as occurred/clear/partial and close
**Test plan:** no tests — observation ticket
